D_phrase: HTTP delay at caller travel-plan produces no intrinsic slow-span on the hosting service and a loud secondary 90s GC pause appears on a downstream queue-fed service.
TimeSkew on the target pod produces no latency, CPU, or error signature at any span; symptoms are purely application-logic-level.
JVM-level MySQL latency injection is masked by a crowd of high-magnitude environmental noises (rabbitmq DNS, consign NonUniqueResultException, order-service DB tail) all firing simultaneously.

Request-side HTTP delay on travel2->seat leaves a literal connection-time signature exactly matching the injection magnitude on travel2, competing with a dramatic worker-node load spike on an unrelated pod.
Container-kill produces both a local pod-restart signal and a correlated-in-time MySQL connection-abort burst (the pod's own connections being torn down), offering two equally-visible explanations.

Container-kill-like perturbation on food-service is masked by the permanent rabbitmq-DNS ambient noise plus a loud GC avalanche on order-service, while the target service itself has a subtle signature.

NetworkChaos on an egress edge produces a caller-selective failure pattern (callee responds fine to other callers) but this asymmetry is only visible by comparing caller-distribution across trace subsets.
PodChaos on ui-dashboard and food-service produces subtle signatures while a mid-stack CPU hotspot on seat-service is dramatic; rabbitmq-DNS noise masks food-service further.
Environment has permanent rabbitmq-DNS misconfiguration always emitting UnknownHostException errors, producing the loudest unambiguous unavailability signature available.
Preserve-service injection amplifies the ambient rabbitmq-DNS signature while preserve's own signal is subtle.
Response-body corruption at route-plan->travel2 leaves no latency/error signature on travel2's server spans; the only artifact is NPEs in route-plan's client code.
DNSRandom fault produces sporadic connection resets and retries on the target's DB client but no single loud anomaly; DNS layer is not directly observable via the agent's tools.
HTTP response delay at travel-plan->train-service is visible only as slow outgoing calls from travel-plan; downstream services on the same trace also look slow by propagation.
JVMMemoryStress at travel-plan makes the injected service slow in a way indistinguishable from 'its downstream is slow' from the trace perspective; a correlated worker-node load spike appears nearby.
Container-kill on food-service is masked by the chronic rabbitmq-DNS noise which amplifies whenever food-service is perturbed in any way.
Response-body corruption on food-service->travel-service leaves no localized anomaly, only decodable garbage at various points.
Container-kill on preserve-service produces a short restart window where preserve's own outgoing calls fail, visible in traces as A->B edge failures originating at A.
HTTP request abort on route-plan->travel2 leaves route-plan as the side with the client-side anomaly; travel2 just sees fewer successful calls.
JVMReturn at station-service produces semantically-wrong but structurally-correct outputs; downstream route-service is the first place the error becomes visible.
JVMException at travel-plan's controller produces 500 responses that propagate as client-visible errors to services travel-plan calls downstream.
Request-side HTTP delay at travel-plan->seat leaves the actual caller travel-plan with slow outgoing spans indistinguishable from 'seat is slow downstream'.
JVMException at order-other's refresh endpoint produces a literal RuntimeException signature in logs; a dramatic but coincidental memory-growth pattern on seat-service competes for attention.
Bandwidth limit on preserve->travel edge makes calls hang, and the hang propagates one more level into travel's internal calls (to basic) which also appear slow by propagation.
Clean HTTP delay on preserve->seat is drowned by preserve's retry cascade amplifying the ambient rabbitmq-DNS noise.
NetworkCorrupt on route-plan->travel-plan produces garbled responses; downstream siblings of travel-plan (like seat) inherit elevated latency through propagation.
HTTP response delay on route-plan->travel leaves route-service (a co-downstream of route-plan) as a plausible but causally-unrelated slow-looking candidate.
Bandwidth limit on seat->config cascades through seat's callers (travel, travel2, route-plan) making them all look slow.
NetworkDelay on seat->travel-plan edge produces diffuse latency spread across multiple consumers; no single service is obviously the source.
Bandwidth on station->basic edge makes basic appear slow to many callers; travel-service is one such observer.
NetworkLoss on travel-plan->ui-dashboard egress edge has no precedent in agent's fault model; peers of travel-plan show ordinary latency variance.
Response-body replacement on travel->basic leaves noisy signatures across many neighboring services but no single dominant signal.
HTTP request abort on travel2->seat cascades through basic-service (a downstream of seat) which also appears slow.
Response-body replacement on travel2->basic corrupts data visible at seat-service (which uses basic), making seat look anomalous.
Response-code replacement on food->train-food is drowned by chronic rabbitmq-DNS noise plus a seat-service CPU hotspot elsewhere on the call graph.
JVMException at preserve cascades into food/notification/delivery retries, amplifying the chronic rabbitmq DNS noise into the loudest visible pattern.
NetworkPartition on preserve->seat triggers the same rabbitmq-noise amplification cascade via preserve's downstream dependencies.
HTTP response delay on preserve->security is one of several latency candidates; preserve->price is also a loud path in the same trace.
Bandwidth limit on security->mysql makes MySQL-adjacent metrics (container.cpu.time) plateau and appear frozen because it is starved of requests, not paused.
NetworkDelay on travel-plan->seat injection produces diffuse latency with no dominant signature visible at any single edge.
Response-code replacement on security->order leaves order-other-service (a near-twin of order-service) with ambiguous signals; both are DB-backed and both see some disruption.
HTTP response abort on travel->basic is subtle, while ts-admin-user-service has a 107x CPU spike completely off the SLO call path; shared-node narrative allows bridging the two.
HTTP response delay on travel->basic makes basic's callees appear slow by propagation; seat-service is one such co-downstream candidate.
HTTP response delay on route-plan->travel2 leaves travel-service (near-twin of travel2 and co-downstream of route-plan) as a structurally-symmetric alternative.
JVMLatency at config-service makes config intrinsically slow; its callers (including basic-service) also appear slow on their config calls.
NetworkPartition on basic->travel produces cascading failures through travel's downstream siblings like seat-service.
Bandwidth limit on basic->preserve (possibly reverse-labeled) produces diffuse signatures; seat-service has the most CPU-anomalous pattern.
Response-code replacement on basic->route makes basic's other downstream (price-service) idle because basic stops calling it; price's metrics appear frozen.
DEFERRED: dataset labeling mismatch; DB meta says ts-config-service, datapack name says ts-food-service-container-kill. Not a genuine agent error.
Bandwidth limit on route-plan->travel makes travel2 (near-twin of travel, co-downstream of route-plan) appear in the symmetric position.
Response-body replacement on seat->order-other amplifies rabbitmq noise through order-other's retry cascade; order-service is a near-twin of order-other.
Container-kill on travel-plan produces a restart window where its outgoing calls to basic-service fail.
